subject: [PATCH v2 13/14] selftests/mm: add userfaultfd RWP tests

reviews.author: Mike Rapoport
reviews.summary:
Reviewer suggested improving code readability by declaring a constant variable at the top of the function, reducing unnecessary indentation

Reviewer Mike Rapoport questioned whether the write() implementation in the patch is bypassing the intended test logic, specifically noting that it will trigger standard hardware page faults during copy_from_user() instead of the get_user_pages() code path, and suggested using vmsplice() to achieve the desired behavior.

Reviewer Mike Rapoport suggested removing the 'started' variable as its value is immediately returned, making it unnecessary.
reviews.raw_body:
can be const uint64_t and declared at the top of the function to avoid
extra indentation here.

---

Sashiko (https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/cover.1778254670.git.kas%40kernel.org?part=13):

	Could this write() implementation be bypassing the intended test
	logic?
	... the write() call here will trigger standard hardware page
	faults during copy_from_user() rather than the intended
	get_user_pages() code path.

It also suggests to use vmsplice().

---

And it seems here we can just return and then started is not needed at
all.

2026-05-13.patch_summary: This patch adds userfaultfd (UFFD) read-write-protect (RWP) tests to the Linux kernel selftest suite. The tests cover various scenarios, including asynchronous and synchronous RWP modes, page mapping, memory protection, and fork operations. The tests verify that UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT work correctly in different situations, ensuring that page permissions are properly restored and protected. The patch includes 11 test cases that cover various types of memory (anon, shmem, hugetlb) and edge cases like fork() and mprotect().
